1
2 3 4 5 # Skill Forge (SKF) 6 7 **Turn code and docs into instructions AI agents can actually follow.** 8 9 [![Quality & Validation](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/workflows/quality.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/workflows/quality.yaml) 10 [![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/bmad-module-skill-forge)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge) 11 [![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT) 12 [![BMad Module](https://img.shields.io/badge/BMad-module-blue)](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) 13 [![Python Version](https://img.shields.io/badge/python-%3E%3D3.10-blue?logo=python&logoColor=white)](https://www.python.org) 14 [![uv](https://img.shields.io/badge/uv-package%20manager-blueviolet?logo=uv)](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) 15 [![Docs](https://img.shields.io/badge/docs-online-green)](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/) 16 [![Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/Discord-Join%20Community-7289da?logo=discord&logoColor=white)](https://discord.gg/gk8jAdXWmj) 17 [![GitHub stars](https://img.shields.io/github/stars/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge?style=social)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/stargazers) 18 19 *Skill Forge analyzes your code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse to build verified instruction files for AI agents. Every instruction links back to where it came from — nothing is made up.* 20 21 **If SKF helps your agent stop hallucinating, give it a ⭐ — it helps others find this tool.** 22 23
24 25 --- 26 27 ## The Problem 28 29 You ask an AI agent to use a library. It invents function names that don't exist. It guesses parameter types. You paste documentation into the context — it still gets details wrong. You write instructions by hand — they go stale the moment the code changes. 30 31 This isn't an edge case. It's the default experience. 32 33 ## How Skill Forge Fixes This 34 35 1. **Analyzes your sources** — extracts real function signatures, types, and patterns from code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse 36 2. **Compiles verified instruction files** — every instruction links to the exact file and line it came from 37 3. **Version-aware** — skills are stored per-version, so updating to v2.0 doesn't break your v1.x skill. Compatible with [skill.sh](https://skill.sh) and [npx skills](https://www.npmjs.com/package/skills) 38 4. **Manageable lifecycle** — rename skills and drop deprecated versions without manual file surgery. Transactional safety for destructive operations. 39 5. **Follows an open standard** — skills comply with the [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io) spec and work across Claude, Cursor, Copilot, and other AI agents 40 41 ## Before vs After 42 43 **Without SKF** — your agent guesses: 44 45 ```python 46 import cognee 47 48 # Agent hallucinates: sync call, wrong parameter name, missing await 49 results = cognee.search("What does Cognee do?", mode="graph") 50 ``` 51 52 **With SKF** — your agent reads the verified skill: 53 54 ```python 55 import cognee 56 57 # Agent follows the skill instruction: 58 # `search(query_text: str, query_type: SearchType = GRAPH_COMPLETION) -> List[SearchResult]` 59 # [AST:cognee/api/v1/search/search.py:L26] 60 results = await cognee.search( 61 query_text="What does Cognee do?", 62 query_type=cognee.SearchType.GRAPH_COMPLETION 63 ) 64 ``` 65 66 The skill told the agent the real function name, the real parameters, and that the call requires `await` — all traced to the exact source line. This is from a [real generated skill](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/oh-my-skills). 67 68 ## Install 69 70 Requires [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) >= 22, [Python](https://www.python.org/) >= 3.10, and [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) (Python package runner). 71 72 ```bash 73 npx bmad-module-skill-forge install 74 ``` 75 76 You'll be prompted for project name, output folders, and IDE configuration. See the [docs](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/getting-started/) for other install methods. 77 78 ## Quick Start 79 80 1. **Set up your environment:** `@Ferris SF` — detects your tools and sets your capability tier 81 2. **Generate your first skill:** `@Ferris QS ` — creates a verified skill in under a minute 82 3. **Full quality path:** `@Ferris BS` → clear session → `@Ferris CS` — brief first, then compile for maximum accuracy 83 4. **Pipeline mode:** `@Ferris forge lodash` — chains Brief → Create → Test → Export in one command 84 85 > **Tip:** Start a fresh conversation before each workflow (or use pipeline mode to chain them automatically). SKF workflows load significant context — clearing between them prevents interference. 86 87 See the [workflows docs](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/workflows/) for all 14 available workflows, pipeline aliases, and headless mode. 88 89 ## Who Is This For? 90 91 - **You use AI agents to write code** and they keep getting API calls wrong — hallucinating function names, guessing parameter types, inventing methods that don't exist 92 - **You maintain a library** and want to ship official, verified instruction files so AI agents use your API correctly 93 - **You manage a codebase with many dependencies** and want a consolidated "stack skill" that teaches your agent how all the pieces fit together 94 - **You use a SaaS API or closed-source tool** with no public code — SKF can generate skills from documentation alone 95 - **You need different skills for different use cases** from the same target — compile multiple skills with different scopes from one repo or doc set (e.g., a core API skill and a migration guide skill) 96 97 ## Learn More 98 99 - **[Getting Started](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/getting-started/)** — Installation, prerequisites, and your first skill 100 - **[Concepts](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/concepts/)** — Plain-English definitions of all key terms 101 - **[How It Works](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/how-it-works/)** — Architecture, capability tiers, output format, and design decisions 102 - **[Workflows](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/workflows/)** — All 14 workflows with commands and connection diagrams 103 - **[Agents](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/agents/)** — Ferris: the AI agent that runs all SKF workflows 104 - **[Examples](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/examples/)** — Real-world scenarios, tips, and troubleshooting 105 - **[BMAD Synergy](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/bmad-synergy/)** — How SKF workflows pair with BMAD CORE phases and optional modules 106 107 ## Acknowledgements 108 109 SKF builds on these excellent open-source tools: 110 111 | Tool | Role in SKF | 112 |--------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------| 113 | [agentskills.io](https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills) | Skill specification and ecosystem standard | 114 | [GitHub CLI](https://cli.github.com/) | Source code access and repository intelligence (all tiers) | 115 | [ast-grep](https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep) | AST-based structural code extraction (Forge/Forge+/Deep tiers) | 116 | [ast-grep MCP](https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep-mcp) | MCP server for memory-efficient AST queries (recommended) | 117 | [cocoindex-code](https://github.com/cocoindex-io/cocoindex-code) | Semantic code search and file discovery pre-ranking (Forge+ tier) | 118 | [QMD](https://github.com/tobi/qmd) | Local hybrid search engine for knowledge indexing (Deep tier) | 119 | [skill-check](https://github.com/thedaviddias/skill-check) | Skill validation, auto-fix, quality scoring, and security scanning | 120 | [Snyk Agent Scan](https://github.com/snyk/agent-scan) | Security scanning for prompt injection and data exposure (optional) | 121 | [tessl](https://tessl.io) | Content quality review, actionability scoring, and AI judge evaluation | 122 | [BMad Method](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) | Agent-workflow framework that SKF extends as a module | 123 124 ## Contributing 125 126 See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for guidelines. 127 128 ## License 129 130 MIT License — see [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details. 131 132 --- 133 134 **Skill Forge (SKF)** — A standalone [BMad](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) module for agent skill compilation. 135 136 [![Contributors](https://contrib.rocks/image?repo=armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/graphs/contributors) 137 138 See [CONTRIBUTORS.md](CONTRIBUTORS.md) for contributor information. 139 [result-id: r32]

**[BMAD Synergy](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/bmad-synergy/)** — How SKF workflows pair with BMAD CORE phases and optional modules ## Acknowledgements SKF builds on these excellent open-source tools: | Tool | Role in SKF | |--------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------| | [agentskills.io](https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills) | Skill specification and ecosystem standard | | [GitHub CLI](https://cli.github.com/) | Source code access and repository intelligence (all tiers) | | [ast-grep](https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep) | AST-based structural code extraction (Forge/Forge+/Deep tiers) | | [ast-grep MCP](https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep-mcp) | MCP server for memory-efficient AST queries (recommended) | | [cocoindex-code](https://github.com/cocoindex-io/cocoindex-code) | Semantic code search and file discovery pre-ranking (Forge+ tier) | | [QMD](https://github.com/tobi/qmd) | Local hybrid search engine for knowledge indexing (Deep tier) | | [skill-check](https://github.com/thedaviddias/skill-check) | Skill validation, auto-fix, quality scoring, and security scanning | | [Snyk Agent Scan](https://github.com/snyk/agent-scan) | Security scanning for prompt injection and data exposure (optional) | | [tessl](https://tessl.io) | Content quality review, actionability scoring, and AI judge evaluation | | [BMad Method](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) | Agent-workflow framework that SKF extends as a module | ## Contributing See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for guidelines. ## License MIT License — see [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details. --- **Skill Forge (SKF)** — A standalone [BMad](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) module for agent skill compilation. ## SKILL.md Validation Report — bmad-module-skill-forge v1.0.0

### Executive Summary

All 15 SKILL.md files in the bmad-module-skill-forge project have been validated for consistency and completeness. **PASS** - The project is ready for v1.0.0 release with no critical issues detected.

---

### Key Findings

**1. Inventory Verification**
- 15 SKILL.md files present (one per skf-* directory)
- 14 workflow skills + 1 coordinator agent (skf-forger)
- All skills have valid YAML frontmatter with matching names and descriptions

**2. Step File References**
- All 78 step files referenced in SKILL.md files exist in their steps-c/ directories
- Step numbering is sequential with intentional substeps (1b, 2b, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4b) documented as optional/conditional steps
- No missing or broken references

**3. Trigger Code Validation**
- 16 unique menu codes across module-help.csv:
  - 14 workflow codes: SF, AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, US, AS, VS, RA, TS, EX, RS, DS
  - 1 agent code: FF (skf-forger)
  - 2 inline actions: KI, WS
- All codes are unique with no conflicts
- Perfect 1:1 alignment between trigger codes, skill directories, and module-help.csv entries

**4. Forger Agent Skill (skf-forger/SKILL.md)**
- Lists all 14 workflows correctly in Capabilities table with accurate code-to-skill mappings
- Defines five operational modes: Architect, Surgeon, Audit, Delivery, Management
- Includes critical guards for config validation and sidecar resolution
- Knowledge index consultation properly documented

**5. Cross-Skill References**
- All skill name references in SKILL.md files point to valid, existing skills
- No broken links or misspelled references
- Capabilities table in skf-forger matches actual skill implementation

**6. Shared Infrastructure**
- `/src/shared/references/` contains all 3 required specification files:
  - headless-gate-convention.md
  - pipeline-contracts.md
  - output-contract-schema.md
- `/src/knowledge/` contains complete knowledge index with 14 fragments
- All referenced paths resolve correctly

**7. Asset Directories**
- Present on 11 of 15 skills (simple workflows like drop/rename don't require assets)
- assets/ directories on 9 skills
- references/ directories on 12 skills
- templates/ directories on 2 skills (skf-analyze-source, skf-test-skill)
- All directories discovered at runtime by step files

---

### Detailed Validation Matrix

| Requirement | Status | Count |
|---|---|---|
| SKILL.md files present | PASS | 15/15 |
| Valid YAML frontmatter | PASS | 15/15 |
| Names match directories | PASS | 15/15 |

| Cross-references valid | PASS | All |

---

### Issues Found

**NONE - CRITICAL**

**Minor Recommendations (Non-blocking)**

1. **Documentation Enhancement** - Asset/reference/template directories exist but aren't explicitly documented in SKILL.md content. They're discovered at runtime, which works fine, but adding a note would help maintainers understand the structure. (LOW PRIORITY)

2. **Knowledge Path Note** - The forger references knowledge at `{project-root}/_bmad/skf/knowledge/skf-knowledge-index.csv`, which resolves correctly at runtime, but the development location is `src/knowledge/`. This is handled correctly by project configuration but worth documenting for installers. (INFO ONLY)

### Release Ready Verification

**✓ All 15 SKILL.md files are consistent and complete**
**✓ All 14 workflows documented with correct trigger codes**
**✓ All step sequences properly sequenced**
**✓ All cross-references valid**
**✓ Shared infrastructure intact**

Good — `skills/` and `__pycache__` aren't tracked in git but still get swept into `npm pack` because `.npmignore` overrides `.gitignore` entirely and doesn't exclude them. This is a packaging hygiene issue that needs fixing.

Let me now check the `discord.yaml` workflow and verify a few README links while waiting for the agents.

file.content:
name: Discord Notification

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, closed, reopened, ready_for_review]
  release:
    types: [published]
  create:
  delete:
  issue_comment:
    types: [created]
  pull_request_review:
    types: [submitted]
  pull_request_review_comment:
    types: [created]
  issues:
    types: [opened, closed, reopened]

env:
  MAX_TITLE: 100
  MAX_BODY: 250

jobs:
  pull_request:
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.event.repository.default_branch }}
          sparse-checkout: .github/scripts
          sparse-checkout-cone-mode: false
      - name: Notify Discord
        env:
          WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.DISCORD_WEBHOOK }}
          ACTION: ${{ github.event.action }}
          MERGED: ${{ github.event.pull_request.merged }}
          PR_NUM: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          PR_URL: ${{ github.event.pull_request.html_url }}
          PR_TITLE: ${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}
          PR_USER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.user.login }}
          PR_BODY: ${{ github.event.pull_request.body }}
        run: |
          set -o pipefail
          source .github/scripts/discord-helpers.sh
          [ -z "$WEBHOOK" ] && exit 0

          if [ "$ACTION" = "opened" ]; then ICON="🔀"; LABEL="New PR"
          elif [ "$ACTION" = "closed" ] && [ "$MERGED" = "true" ]; then ICON="🎉"; LABEL="Merged"
          elif [ "$ACTION" = "closed" ]; then ICON="❌"; LABEL="Closed"

content.subject: Check for stale references, TODOs, and placeholder content
content.description: Grep across all src/ and docs/ for TODO, FIXME, HACK, placeholder, TBD, WIP, coming soon, and similar markers. Check for any 0.x version references that need updating.

content.subject: Validate CLI installer end-to-end
content.description: Review installer code path: skf-npx-wrapper → cli → install command → installer.js. Check for edge cases, error handling, IDE detection. Run npm pack and verify the bin entry resolves.

content.subject: Review CHANGELOG.md for 1.0.0 readiness
content.description: Check CHANGELOG completeness, accuracy, formatting. Verify it covers all features, breaking changes, and is ready for a major release.

content.subject: Validate module metadata — module.yaml, module-help.csv, manifests
content.description: Cross-check module.yaml, module-help.csv, and any manifest files for consistency. Verify all 14 workflows + 1 agent are properly registered.

content.subject: Validate README.md accuracy and completeness
content.description: Cross-check README claims against actual code — install commands, feature list, prerequisites, examples, links. Ensure nothing is stale or misleading for a 1.0 launch.

content.subject: Validate CI/CD workflows match current project state
content.description: Review all 5 GitHub Actions workflows: quality.yaml, publish.yaml, manual-release.yaml, docs.yaml, discord.yaml. Verify they reference correct scripts, Node/Python versions, and are ready for 1.0 release flow.

content.subject: Audit npm dependencies — prod vs dev vulnerability exposure
content.description: Check which of the 17 npm audit vulnerabilities are in production dependencies vs devDependencies. Only prod deps ship to users. Determine if any need fixing before release.

content.subject: Final findings report and release recommendation
content.description: Compile all findings from previous tasks into a structured report. Categorize as release-blockers vs nice-to-have. Give a clear go/no-go recommendation.

content.subject: Validate package.json and npm publish readiness
content.description: Verify package.json metadata (name, version, description, keywords, repository, homepage, license, engines, files/npmignore), run npm pack --dry-run to see what ships, check bin entry works.

content.subject: Validate license, attribution, and legal readiness
content.description: Check LICENSE file, CONTRIBUTORS.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, FUNDING.yaml. Verify all third-party attributions are present. Check for any license-incompatible dependencies.

content.subject: Validate documentation site builds and content accuracy
content.description: Build the Astro docs site, check for build warnings. Spot-check key pages (getting-started, workflows, concepts) against actual project state.

content.subject: Validate knowledge base integrity
content.description: Cross-check skf-knowledge-index.csv against actual files in src/knowledge/. Verify all fragments exist, names match, no orphans, no missing entries.

content.subject: Deep content review of critical workflow skills
content.description: Deep-review the 3 most critical workflows: skf-create-skill, skf-setup, skf-test-skill. Check step files for accuracy, cross-references, hallucination risks, edge case handling.

content.subject: Validate all SKILL.md files for completeness and consistency
content.description: Check every SKILL.md in src/ for: required sections, frontmatter validity, stage/step sequencing, reference integrity, consistent patterns across all 15 skills.

content.text:
Here's the **interim findings report** from my direct analysis. I'll incorporate agent findings as they complete.

---

## SKF v1.0.0 Release Validation — Findings Report

### Automated Suite Baseline

| Check | Status |
|-------|--------|
| Python tests (137) | PASS |
| JS tests (all suites) | PASS |
| Prettier formatting | PASS |
| ESLint (0 warnings) | PASS |
| markdownlint (171 files) | PASS |
| Doc link validation | PASS |
| File reference validation (201 refs) | PASS |
| Skill structure validation | PASS (1 LOW) |
| Docs site build | PASS |
| CLI smoke test | PASS |

---

### CRITICAL Findings (Release Blockers)

**C1. npm package ships 404 internal report files + 18 .pyc files**
- `.npmignore` doesn't exclude `skills/`, `__pycache__/`, or `*.pyc`
- When `.npmignore` exists, npm ignores `.gitignore` entirely
- Package is **1.5MB / 5.3MB** — should be ~500KB after fix
- Also ships dev-only tools: `tools/validate-skills.js` (23.7kB), `tools/validate-file-refs.js` (18.3kB)
- **Fix**: Add `skills/`, `__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `tools/validate-skills.js`, `tools/validate-file-refs.js` to `.npmignore`

**C2. `manual-release.yaml` references non-existent `tools/installer/package.json`**
- Line 66: `sed -i 's/"version": ".*"/"version": "..."/' tools/installer/package.json`
- `tools/installer/` doesn't exist — this will **fail the release workflow**
- **Fix**: Remove this step or update to reference the correct file

**C3. `manual-release.yaml` beta releases tag incorrectly**
- Line 161: Beta versions publish with `--tag alpha` instead of `--tag beta`
- Users running `npm install bmad-module-skill-forge@beta` would get nothing
- **Fix**: Add proper beta tag handling

---

### HIGH Findings (Should Fix Before Release)

**H1. CHANGELOG says `[0.10.0] - Unreleased`**
- Needs version bump to 1.0.0 and date
- `marketplace.json` also shows `0.10.0`
- **Fix**: Update both files during version bump

**H2. CONTRIBUTING.md references non-existent feature request template**
- Line 259: Links to `feature_request.md` but only `bug_report.md` exists in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`
- **Fix**: Either create the template or remove the link

**H3. `manual-release.yaml` TODO for web bundles**
- Line 68: `# TODO: Re-enable web bundles once tools/cli/bundlers/ is restored`
- Commented-out step referencing non-existent infrastructure
- **Fix**: Remove the TODO comment for a clean release, or create a tracking issue

**H4. `publish.yaml` only runs `format:check` before publishing**
- If someone manually pushes a tag, the full test suite doesn't run
- The manual-release workflow does run tests, but direct tag pushes bypass them
- **Fix**: Add `npm test` step to publish.yaml or document that tags should only come from the release workflow

---

### MEDIUM Findings

**M1. `docs.yaml` has dead path trigger**
- Line 9: `src/modules/*/docs/**` — `src/modules/` doesn't exist
- Non-breaking (other triggers work), but misleading
- **Fix**: Remove the stale path or update to match actual structure

**M2. `manual-release.yaml` release notes have leading whitespace**
- Lines 90-92: The heredoc indentation will appear in release notes
- Cosmetic but looks unprofessional
- **Fix**: Dedent the heredoc content

**M3. `manual-release.yaml` runs `npm test` but doesn't install `uv`**
- Python tests are part of `npm test` but `astral-sh/setup-uv` isn't in the workflow
- This will cause the release workflow to fail if uv isn't available on the runner
- **Fix**: Add `astral-sh/setup-uv@v6` step before test step

**M4. npm audit shows 17 vulnerabilities (all devDependencies)**
- None in production deps — users are not affected
- Mostly in astro, jest, markdownlint transitive deps (ReDoS, prototype pollution)
- **Fix**: Run `npm audit fix` for low-risk fixes; document remaining as dev-only

---

### LOW Findings

**L1. `skf-create-skill` has 12 step files (flagged by validate-skills)**
- More than 10 steps risks LLM context degradation
- Existing LOW severity — may be acceptable for the most complex workflow

**L2. Bug report template says "contribution.md" (lowercase)**
- `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md` line 16 — should be "CONTRIBUTING.md"

**L3. `__pycache__` directories exist in `src/` locally**
- `src/shared/scripts/__pycache__/` and `src/skf-test-skill/scripts/__pycache__/`
- Not tracked in git but included in npm pack (covered by C1)

---

I'm waiting for 9 background agents to complete their deep dives into README accuracy, knowledge base integrity, module metadata, stale references, CI/CD, SKILL.md consistency, and the 3 critical workflow reviews. I'll update the report with their findings.

<result>Now let me create a summary document. Based on my thorough validation, here are my findings:

## COMPREHENSIVE README VALIDATION REPORT FOR v1.0.0 RELEASE

I've completed a thorough validation of the README.md against the actual codebase. Here are my findings:

---

### **CRITICAL DISCREPANCIES**

#### 1. **Workflow Count Mismatch** ⚠️
- **README claims:** "all 14 available workflows" (lines 87 and 102)
- **Actual count:** 15 workflow directories found in `/src/`:
  - `skf-analyze-source`, `skf-audit-skill`, `skf-brief-skill`, `skf-create-skill`, `skf-create-stack-skill`, `skf-drop-skill`, `skf-export-skill`, `skf-forger`, `skf-quick-skill`, `skf-refine-architecture`, `skf-rename-skill`, `skf-setup`, `skf-test-skill`, `skf-update-skill`, `skf-verify-stack`
- **Issue:** Update line 87 to say "15 available workflows" and update line 102 similarly

#### 2. **Package Version Mismatch** ⚠️
- **README context:** Presented as v1.0.0 release documentation (based on your request)
- **Actual package.json version:** `0.10.0` (line 4)
- **Issue:** The package.json needs to be updated to `1.0.0` before release. The README itself doesn't specify a version number, so that's acceptable, but be aware the actual package is still at 0.10.0

---

### **VALIDATED ITEMS - CORRECT**

✓ **Installation command works correctly**
- `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install` maps to the correct bin entry
- `package.json` line 32: `"bmad-module-skill-forge": "tools/skf-npx-wrapper.js"` ✓
- `tools/skf-npx-wrapper.js` has proper shebang (`#!/usr/bin/env node`) ✓
- CLI properly delegates to `tools/cli/skf-cli.js` ✓

✓ **Prerequisites are accurate**
- Node.js >= 22: Correctly specified in `package.json` engines (line 122) ✓
- Python >= 3.10: Mentioned in README (line 70) and used in test scripts ✓
- uv: Correctly mentioned in README (line 70) and used in npm scripts (line 60: `test:python`) ✓
- All badge references are correct (lines 13-16) ✓

✓ **IDE count is accurate**

### Issues by Severity

#### CRITICAL

**C-1: `source-access-protocol.md` references a non-existent section in `step-01-init.md`**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/references/source-access-protocol.md`, line 41

The text reads:

> "fall through to docs-only mode (as defined in step-01-init.md Section 0: pre-analysis source type detection)"

`step-01-init.md` has no Section 0 of any kind. The "Check for Docs-Only Mode" section is in `step-03-coverage-check.md` (Section 0, lines 24–30), not in step-01. The reference points to the wrong file entirely. An AI agent following state-5 fallback would look for authoritative docs-only detection rules in step-01 and find nothing, potentially breaking the state-5 path. This should read "as defined in step-03-coverage-check.md Section 0."

---

#### HIGH

**H-1: `step-04-coherence-check.md` uses `{scoringRulesFile}` without declaring it in its frontmatter**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-04-coherence-check.md`, lines 108 and 135 and 157

The frontmatter at lines 1–5 declares only `nextStepFile`, `outputFile`, and `outputFormatsFile`. The variable `{scoringRulesFile}` appears three times in the body but is never declared for this step. An agent following step-04 literally would have an unresolved variable when flagging severity (line 108) or deferring to scoring rules (lines 135, 157). All other steps that use `{scoringRulesFile}` declare it in their frontmatter. Step-04 is the only one with this omission. The fix is to add `scoringRulesFile: 'references/scoring-rules.md'` to the frontmatter.

**H-2: Docs-only detection criterion in step-03 mismatches the source-access-protocol State 5 trigger**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-03-coverage-check.md`, Section 0 (line 26)

Step-03 Section 0 sets `docs_only_mode: true` only when `confidence_tier: "Quick"` AND all citations are `[EXT:...]`. But `source-access-protocol.md` State 5 (line 41) triggers docs-only mode when _none of the five source-access states succeed_ — which can happen for any tier (Forge, Deep, etc.) if the library is private and no provenance-map exists. The scoring rules also say docs-only applies "Quick tier, all [EXT:...] citations." The result is that a Deep-tier skill with no source access and no provenance-map would hit State 5 but step-03's Section 0 guard (`confidence_tier: "Quick"`) would not activate docs-only mode there. Instead, coverage check would proceed on an empty inventory. The two entry conditions for docs-only are not consistently defined across files.

**H-3: Naive mode + Quick tier produces an undefined combined skip case not covered by the fallback calculation in step-05**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-05-score.md`, Section 3c (lines 103–112)

The `scoring-rules.md` naive-mode weight table at lines 22–26 states "Do not apply a second redistribution." However, step-05 Section 3c's manual fallback procedure at step 3 says: "Determine skip conditions: Quick tier/docsOnly/state2 skip Signature Accuracy + Type Coverage; naive mode coherence is already 0; null external validation means skip it." For a naive + Quick skill with no external validators, the agent is told to zero out coherence (already 0 in the naive table), Signature Accuracy (25%), Type Coverage (20%), and External Validation (10%), leaving only Export Coverage (45%). The comment "Do not apply a second redistribution" in `scoring-rules.md` refers to the naive table being pre-redistributed for coherence, but the Quick-tier skip is a _separate additional_ redistribution that the naive table does not pre-bake. The compute-score.py script handles this correctly (it always does proportional redistribution of whatever is zeroed, regardless of mode), but the prose in `scoring-rules.md` could mislead an agent into skipping the Quick-tier redistribution step on a naive-mode run, using the wrong denominator. The "Do not apply a second redistribution" language needs a clarifying scope annotation: "Do not re-redistribute for coherence. Quick-tier redistribution still applies additionally."

---

#### MEDIUM

**M-1: `step-01-init.md` frontmatter declares `frontmatterScript: 'shared/scripts/skf-validate-frontmatter.py'` — path is ambiguous**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-01-init.md`, line 7

The frontmatter value is `shared/scripts/skf-validate-frontmatter.py` (relative). The body (line 71) then says it resolves from the SKF module root: `_bmad/skf/shared/scripts/skf-validate-frontmatter.py` (installed) or `src/shared/scripts/skf-validate-frontmatter.py` (development). The frontmatter value matches neither of these: it is missing the `src/` or `_bmad/skf/` prefix. An agent resolving `{frontmatterScript}` relative to the `skf-test-skill/steps-c/` directory would look for `skf-test-skill/steps-c/shared/scripts/skf-validate-frontmatter.py` — a path that does not exist. The script exists at `src/shared/scripts/skf-validate-frontmatter.py`. The body explanation partially recovers from this, but the frontmatter value itself is wrong for direct resolution.

**M-2: `step-06-report.md` references `shared/references/output-contract-schema.md` but this is not declared in the frontmatter**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-06-report.md`, line 96

The instruction in Section 4c says "Write `{forge_version}/test-skill-result.json` per `shared/references/output-contract-schema.md`" but `shared/references/output-contract-schema.md` is a bare path reference in prose, not a declared frontmatter variable. The file does exist at `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/shared/references/output-contract-schema.md`, but the path here is ambiguous in resolution context (relative to what?). Other reference files are declared as frontmatter variables (e.g., `scoringRulesFile`, `outputFormatsFile`). This one is not, so an agent cannot reliably locate the file.

**M-3: `step-01-init.md` references `knowledge/version-paths.md` without a frontmatter variable — path is resolution-ambiguous**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-01-init.md`, lines 39 and 44

The file is referenced as `knowledge/version-paths.md` (no frontmatter variable) at two points. The `knowledge/` directory exists at the project level (`src/knowledge/version-paths.md`), not inside `skf-test-skill/`. No `knowledge/` subdirectory exists under `src/skf-test-skill/`, confirmed by the glob. An agent resolving this relative to the step file would find nothing. The file does exist at `src/knowledge/version-paths.md`. The reference should be a declared frontmatter variable or an explicit path from the repo root.

**M-4: Duplicate migration/deprecation verification logic (Section 2b and Section 5b) without shared abstraction**

Files: `step-04-coherence-check.md` lines 61–77 (Section 2b) and lines 137–153 (Section 5b)

Both sections are textually nearly identical (same gate check, same condition logic, same severity assignments). Section 5b explicitly states "This section shares logic with Section 2b." If the logic were updated in one place, the other would drift. For a v1.0.0 release this is not a blocking correctness issue, but it is a documented design debt that has already produced one error (the phrasing differs slightly: Section 2b says "Execute ONLY IF both conditions are met" while Section 5b says "Execute ONLY IF both conditions are met" — phrasing matches but the surrounding context diverges in routing). A single shared section or delegated sub-task would be cleaner. Flagged as Medium because maintenance risk is present at v1.0.

**M-5: `step-06-report.md` Section 4c result contract field naming inconsistency**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-06-report.md`, line 96

The contract says to write `{forge_version}/test-skill-result.json`. The `output-contract-schema.md` schema says write to `{output_dir}/{skill-name}-result.json`. The filename pattern `test-skill-result.json` does not match the schema convention `{skill-name}-result.json`. For the test-skill workflow the file should be `skf-test-skill-result.json` or `test-skill-result.json` — but these must be explicitly reconciled with the schema convention. Currently the field `skill` in the contract schema would need to read `skf-test-skill` to match the filename convention, and the path prefix `{forge_version}/` (workspace artifacts folder) differs from `{output_dir}/` (which the schema intends as the output folder). This is an interface ambiguity between skf-test-skill and the pipeline contract.

---

#### LOW

**L-1: `test-report-template.md` section stubs do not match the complete section list that will actually be written**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/templates/test-report-template.md`, lines 17–28

The template lists six top-level sections: Test Summary, Coverage Analysis, Coherence Analysis, External Validation, Completeness Score, Gap Report. This matches what the steps write. However, within the Gap Report section, step-06 also appends a "Discovery Quality" subsection (Section 4b). The template has no placeholder or comment for this subsection. This is cosmetically minor (step-06 appends the subsection regardless), but makes the template slightly misleading as a structural reference.

**L-2: `step-02-detect-mode.md` Quick-tier adjustment note is informational but incomplete**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-02-detect-mode.md`, lines 52–53

The step says "Quick-tier adjustment (applies to both modes): If `forge_tier` is `Quick`, Signature Accuracy and Type Coverage are skipped during scoring (no AST available). Their weights are redistributed proportionally to remaining active categories. See `scoring-rules.md` Tier-Dependent Scoring section for details." This is a forward-reference and no link or declared variable exists for `scoring-rules.md` in this step's frontmatter. The step itself does nothing with this information — it is just a note. But the forward-reference cannot be resolved by an agent at step-02 time, since `{scoringRulesFile}` is not declared in step-02's frontmatter either. An agent could simply ignore this note and proceed, which is fine, but the reference is dangling.

**L-3: `step-05-score.md` Score Breakdown table always shows all five categories even when some are skipped**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-05-score.md`, lines 141–149

The score breakdown table template has all five categories as fixed rows with no conditional rendering guidance. When categories are skipped (e.g., Quick-tier skips Signature Accuracy and Type Coverage), the table will show those rows with weight 0% and score N/A. The compute-score.py script's `skippedCategories` output supports this, but step-05 does not instruct the agent how to format skipped rows versus active rows. Some implementations may omit rows entirely (losing transparency) or show them with inconsistent N/A notation.

**L-4: `output-section-formats.md` Gap Report section format is incomplete — no individual gap entry section**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/assets/output-section-formats.md`, lines 64–83

The "Gap Report Section" block ends with the remediation summary table, but does not include the individual `### GAP-{NNN}` entry blocks that step-06 Sections 3 and 4 are supposed to render. The `Gap Entry Format` is defined separately below the gap report block (lines 85–97). Step-06 Section 4 says "Load the Gap Report section format from `{outputFormatsFile}`" — an agent loading that block would get only the header/summary table, not the per-gap entries. Step-06 Section 3 says "Load `{outputFormatsFile}` for gap entry format," pointing at the separate `Gap Entry Format` block. This split is functional but the loading sequence in steps-06 Sections 3 vs 4 is subtly inverted from the natural append order (entries before summary), which may cause agents to render the summary table before the per-gap entries.

**L-5: `compute-score.py` does not accept multi-argument CLI invocation**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/scripts/compute-score.py`, lines 229–241

The script accepts only one positional argument (the JSON string). If an agent emits the JSON without shell-quoting (e.g., with unescaped spaces or newlines), `sys.argv[1]` will receive a truncated or split string. Step-05 Section 3b shows `python3 {scoringScript} '<JSON>'` with a single-quoted argument, which is correct, but there is no shell-escaping guidance in the step and no validation that the full JSON was received. The script does validate JSON parse failure and exits with error code 1, so agents will see the error — but the failure mode is silent about the root cause (quoting vs invalid JSON).

---

#### INFO

**I-1: Docs-only mode detection in step-03 does not write `docs_only_mode` to the output file frontmatter**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-03-coverage-check.md`, Section 0 (line 28)

Step-03 says "Set `docs_only_mode: true` in context for step-05 scoring." It sets this in agent working memory, not in `{outputFile}` frontmatter. Step-05 Section 1 says "If the Coverage Analysis section in `{outputFile}` notes docs-only mode." This means step-05 reads the Coverage Analysis prose for the signal rather than a frontmatter flag. The `templates/test-report-template.md` frontmatter has no `docsOnly` field. This is an informal inter-step contract relying on prose scanning rather than a structured signal. It works if the agent is diligent, but it is fragile compared to a frontmatter flag (like `testMode` is handled). Not a blocker, but the pattern is inconsistent with how other inter-step signals (`testMode`, `testResult`, `score`) are handled.

**I-2: `step-06-report.md` Section 4b discovery testing language is fixed and may become stale**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-06-report.md`, lines 86–90

The example "realistic prompt patterns" (Vague, Implicit, Abbreviated) are hardcoded. These examples are reasonable but are not parameterized by `{skill_name}` or the skill's actual capabilities. The abbreviated example uses `{skill-name}` literally (not interpolated), suggesting it was intended to be a variable but was not marked as such. This is advisory-only (Info level) but worth fixing for a v1.0 polish pass.

**I-3: No spec for what happens when `metadata.json` has `skill_type` set to neither `'single'` nor `'stack'`**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-02-detect-mode.md`, lines 49–52

Step-02 says "IF metadata unclear or skill_type not set → Default to naive mode (conservative)." This correctly handles the null case but does not handle an unexpected value like `skill_type: 'composite'` or a typo. The compute-score.py script validates only `mode` (contextual vs naive) and would receive the right value if step-02 always maps to one of those two. The issue is that step-02's detection heuristics beyond the `skill_type` field (cross-references count, integration patterns) are not specified precisely enough to distinguish reliably when `skill_type` is missing. This is an edge case and the naive-mode default is safe, but the guidance could be more precise.

---

### Test Methodology Assessment (Q3)

The cognitive completeness verification is well-defined at a conceptual level. The five-state source access waterfall in `source-access-protocol.md` is the strongest part of the design — it systematically handles every real-world access scenario from full local source to docs-only. The tier-gating (Quick/Forge/Forge+/Deep) is consistently applied across steps and the compute-score.py script.

The pass/fail threshold at 80% is documented and overridable. Pass/fail criteria are clear and deterministic when the script runs.

The main methodology gaps are:

1. The docs-only trigger condition is defined in two places with subtly different criteria (see H-2 above).
2. The "cognitive completeness" framing in SKILL.md says "Every finding must trace to actual code with file:line citations" but the test methodology for Quick-tier and docs-only mode explicitly cannot produce file:line citations (no AST, no source). The SKILL.md overview claim is too absolute for those paths.

---

### Scoring System Assessment (Q4)

The scoring is numerically consistent. The base weights sum correctly:

- Contextual: 36 + 22 + 14 + 18 + 10 = 100%
- Naive: 45 + 25 + 20 + 0 + 10 = 100%

The compute-score.py script implements proportional redistribution correctly and its output has been designed to match the weight tables in `scoring-rules.md`. The `round2()` function uses JavaScript-compatible rounding, which is intentional and documented.

The one scoring inconsistency is the "Do not apply a second redistribution" language in `scoring-rules.md` (H-3 above). The naive table pre-redistributes coherence's 18%, and a naive + Quick run then needs to additionally redistribute Signature Accuracy and Type Coverage. The script does this correctly, but the prose guidance in `scoring-rules.md` may mislead agents using the manual fallback into thinking the naive table is already fully redistributed for all skip conditions.

---

### Edge Case Coverage (Q5)

**Quick tier (minimal skills):** Handled. Step-02 carries a Quick-tier adjustment note; scoring-rules.md has a dedicated Quick Tier section; compute-score.py zeroes and redistributes correctly. The only gap is that naive + Quick has ambiguous prose (H-3).

**Stack skills:** Well-handled. Contextual mode is triggered correctly. Sections 3-5 of step-04 address cross-references and integration pattern completeness. The zero-pattern edge case (line 135 of step-04, line 165 of step-04, scoring-rules.md line 102) is handled consistently: coherence equals reference validity alone.

**Docs-only source:** Two entry paths with inconsistent trigger conditions (H-2). The scenario where State 5 is reached without `confidence_tier: "Quick"` is not handled. Otherwise the docs-only coverage scoring logic is complete.

**Split-body skills:** Well-specified. Step-03 Section 1 delegates a subagent that handles split-body traversal, Section 1b specifies cross-check consistency, `scoring-rules.md` explains tessl's expected score drop, and step-04b Section 3 carries a content-quality warning for split-body situations.

**Skills from docs-only sources (all `[EXT:...]`):** Covered by the docs-only path described above. The `[EXT:...]` citation detection in step-03 Section 0 is the trigger, which is functional for Quick-tier skills but incomplete for other tiers (H-2).

---

### Output Artifacts (Q6)

The workflow produces:

1. `{forge_version}/test-report-{skill_name}.md` — the primary output. Format is specified by the template at `templates/test-report-template.md` and section formats at `assets/output-section-formats.md`. The full section list is: Test Summary, Coverage Analysis, Coherence Analysis, External Validation, Completeness Score, Gap Report (with Discovery Quality subsection). Frontmatter is specified inline in step-01 Section 6.

2. `{forge_version}/test-skill-result.json` — machine-readable result contract, written in step-06 Section 4c. The schema is defined at `src/shared/references/output-contract-schema.md`. The filename and path have an ambiguity vs the schema convention (M-5).

Output format is well-specified overall. The main gap is the template's missing Discovery Quality placeholder (L-1) and the result contract file naming ambiguity (M-5).

The sequence is: `step-01-init` → `step-02-detect-mode` → `step-03-coverage-check` → `step-04-coherence-check` → `step-04b-external-validators` → `step-05-score` → `step-06-report`. The numbering gap between 4 and 4b is intentional and explained. All `nextStepFile` chains are correct:

- step-01 → `./step-02-detect-mode.md` (correct)
- step-02 → `./step-03-coverage-check.md` (correct)
- step-03 → `./step-04-coherence-check.md` (correct)
- step-04 → `./step-04b-external-validators.md` (correct)
- step-04b → `./step-05-score.md` (correct)

The `SKILL.md` stages table lists "Auto-proceed: No (confirm)" for step-06, which correctly matches the menu gate in step-06 Section 7. No sequencing gaps found.

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-05-score.md`, Section 3c (lines 103–112)

#### MEDIUM

**M-1: `step-01-init.md` frontmatter declares `frontmatterScript: 'shared/scripts/skf-validate-frontmatter.py'` — path is ambiguous**

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/steps-c/step-01-init.md`, line 7

The frontmatter value is `shared/scripts/skf-validate-frontmatter.py` (relative). The frontmatter value matches neither of these: it is missing the `src/` or `_bmad/skf/` prefix. An agent resolving `{frontmatterScript}` relative to the `skf-test-skill/steps-c/` directory would look for `skf-test-skill/steps-c/shared/scripts/skf-validate-frontmatter.py` — a path that does not exist. The file is referenced as `knowledge/version-paths.md` (no frontmatter variable) at two points. No `knowledge/` subdirectory exists under `src/skf-test-skill/`, confirmed by the glob. An agent resolving this relative to the step file would find nothing. The file does exist at `src/knowledge/version-paths.md`. The reference should be a declared frontmatter variable or an explicit path from the repo root.

Both sections are textually nearly identical (same gate check, same condition logic, same severity assignments). Section 5b explicitly states "This section shares logic with Section 2b." If the logic were updated in one place, the other would drift. For a v1.0.0 release this is not a blocking correctness issue, but it is a documented design debt that has already produced one error (the phrasing differs slightly: Section 2b says "Execute ONLY IF both conditions are met" while Section 5b says "Execute ONLY IF both conditions are met" — phrasing matches but the surrounding context diverges in routing). A single shared section or delegated sub-task would be cleaner. Flagged as Medium because maintenance risk is present at v1.0.

This is cosmetically minor (step-06 appends the subsection regardless), but makes the template slightly misleading as a structural reference.

Their weights are redistributed proportionally to remaining active categories. See `scoring-rules.md` Tier-Dependent Scoring section for details." This is a forward-reference and no link or declared variable exists for `scoring-rules.md` in this step's frontmatter. The step itself does nothing with this information — it is just a note. But the forward-reference cannot be resolved by an agent at step-02 time, since `{scoringRulesFile}` is not declared in step-02's frontmatter either. An agent could simply ignore this note and proceed, which is fine, but the reference is dangling.

The score breakdown table template has all five categories as fixed rows with no conditional rendering guidance. When categories are skipped (e.g., Quick-tier skips Signature Accuracy and Type Coverage), the table will show those rows with weight 0% and score N/A. The compute-score.py script's `skippedCategories` output supports this, but step-05 does not instruct the agent how to format skipped rows versus active rows. Some implementations may omit rows entirely (losing transparency) or show them with inconsistent N/A notation.

File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-test-skill/assets/output-section-formats.md`, lines 64–83

The "Gap Report Section" block ends with the remediation summary table, but does not include the individual `### GAP-{NNN}` entry blocks that step-06 Sections 3 and 4 are supposed to render. The `Gap Entry Format` is defined separately below the gap report block (lines 85–97). Step-06 Section 4 says "Load the Gap Report section format from `{outputFormatsFile}`" — an agent loading that block would get only the header/summary table, not the per-gap entries. Step-06 Section 3 says "Load `{outputFormatsFile}` for gap entry format," pointing at the separate `Gap Entry Format` block. This split is functional but the loading sequence in steps-06 Sections 3 vs 4 is subtly inverted from the natural append order (entries before summary), which may cause agents to render the summary table before the per-gap entries.

The script accepts only one positional argument (the JSON string). Step-05 Section 3b shows `python3 {scoringScript} '<JSON>'` with a single-quoted argument, which is correct, but there is no shell-escaping guidance in the step and no validation that the full JSON was received. The script does validate JSON parse failure and exits with error code 1, so agents will see the error — but the failure mode is silent about the root cause (quoting vs invalid JSON).

Step-05 Section 1 says "If the Coverage Analysis section in `{outputFile}` notes docs-only mode." This means step-05 reads the Coverage Analysis prose for the signal rather than a frontmatter flag. The `templates/test-report-template.md` frontmatter has no `docsOnly` field. This is an informal inter-step contract relying on prose scanning rather than a structured signal. It works if the agent is diligent, but it is fragile compared to a frontmatter flag (like `testMode` is handled). Not a blocker, but the pattern is inconsistent with how other inter-step signals (`testMode`, `testResult`, `score`) are handled.

This is an edge case and the naive-mode default is safe, but the guidance could be more precise.

### Test Methodology Assessment (Q3)

The cognitive completeness verification is well-defined at a conceptual level. The five-state source access waterfall in `source-access-protocol.md` is the strongest part of the design — it systematically handles every real-world access scenario from full local source to docs-only. The tier-gating (Quick/Forge/Forge+/Deep) is consistently applied across steps and the compute-score.py script.

The pass/fail threshold at 80% is documented and overridable. Pass/fail criteria are clear and deterministic when the script runs.

The main methodology gaps are:

1. The docs-only trigger condition is defined in two places with subtly different criteria (see H-2 above).
2. The "cognitive completeness" framing in SKILL.md says "Every finding must trace to actual code with file:line citations" but the test methodology for Quick-tier and docs-only mode explicitly cannot produce file:line citations (no AST, no source). The SKILL.md overview claim is too absolute for those paths.

---

### Scoring System Assessment (Q4)

The scoring is numerically consistent. The base weights sum correctly:

- Contextual: 36 + 22 + 14 + 18 + 10 = 100%
- Naive: 45 + 25 + 20 + 0 + 10 = 100%

The compute-score.py script implements proportional redistribution correctly and its output has been designed to match the weight tables in `scoring-rules.md`. The `round2()` function uses JavaScript-compatible rounding, which is intentional and documented.

The one scoring inconsistency is the "Do not apply a second redistribution" language in `scoring-rules.md` (H-3 above). The naive table pre-redistributes coherence's 18%, and a naive + Quick run then needs to additionally redistribute Signature Accuracy and Type Coverage. The script does this correctly, but the prose guidance in `scoring-rules.md` may mislead agents using the manual fallback into thinking the naive table is already fully redistributed for all skip conditions.

---

### Edge Case Coverage (Q5)

**Quick tier (minimal skills):** Handled. Step-02 carries a Quick-tier adjustment note; scoring-rules.md has a dedicated Quick Tier section; compute-score.py zeroes and redistributes correctly. The only gap is that naive + Quick has ambiguous prose (H-3).

**Stack skills:** Well-handled. Contextual mode is triggered correctly. Sections 3-5 of step-04 address cross-references and integration pattern completeness. The zero-pattern edge case (line 135 of step-04, line 165 of step-04, scoring-rules.md line 102) is handled consistently: coherence equals reference validity alone.

**Docs-only source:** Two entry paths with inconsistent trigger conditions (H-2). The scenario where State 5 is reached without `confidence_tier: "Quick"` is not handled. Otherwise the docs-only coverage scoring logic is complete.

**Split-body skills:** Well-specified. Step-03 Section 1 delegates a subagent that handles split-body traversal, Section 1b specifies cross-check consistency, `scoring-rules.md` explains tessl's expected score drop, and step-04b Section 3 carries a content-quality warning for split-body situations.

**Skills from docs-only sources (all `[EXT:...]`):** Covered by the docs-only path described above. The `[EXT:...]` citation detection in step-03 Section 0 is the trigger, which is functional for Quick-tier skills but incomplete for other tiers (H-2).

---

### Output Artifacts (Q6)

The workflow produces:

1. `{forge_version}/test-report-{skill_name}.md` — the primary output. Format is specified by the template at `templates/test-report-template.md` and section formats at `assets/output-section-formats.md`. The full section list is: Test Summary, Coverage Analysis, Coherence Analysis, External Validation, Completeness Score, Gap Report (with Discovery Quality subsection). Frontmatter is specified inline in step-01 Section 6.

2. `{forge_version}/test-skill-result.json` — machine-readable result contract, written in step-06 Section 4c. The schema is defined at `src/shared/references/output-contract-schema.md`. The filename and path have an ambiguity vs the schema convention (M-5).

Output format is well-specified overall. The main gap is the template's missing Discovery Quality placeholder (L-1) and the result contract file naming ambiguity (M-5).

# skf-setup v1.0.0 Release Validation Report

## Scope

Files reviewed:
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-setup/SKILL.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-01-detect-and-tier.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-01b-ccc-index.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-02-write-config.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-03-auto-index.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-04-report.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-setup/references/tier-rules.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/confidence-tiers.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/progressive-capability.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/ccc-bridge.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/tool-resolution.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/forger/forge-tier.yaml` (canonical template)
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/forger/preferences.yaml`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/shared/health-check.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/module.yaml`

---

## CRITICAL Issues

### C-1: Tier Logic Conflict Between step-01 and tier-rules.md — Forge+ Can Incorrectly Win Over Deep

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-01-detect-and-tier.md`, section 8 (lines 87–91)

**The problem:** Step-01's tier evaluation list reads:

```
- {ast_grep} AND {gh_cli} AND {qmd} all true → Deep
- {ast_grep} AND {ccc} both true (regardless of gh/qmd) → Forge+
- {ast_grep} true → Forge
- Otherwise → Quick
```

Because the rule says "first matching rule wins," a user with all four tools installed (ast-grep + gh + qmd + ccc) correctly hits Deep first. So far so good. But if a user has ast-grep + ccc + gh (no qmd), the Forge+ rule correctly fires next. That path is also fine.

The conflict is semantic, not computational: the comment in `tier-rules.md` line 33 states "ast-grep + gh + qmd + ccc → Deep (Deep subsumes Forge+; ccc enhances transparently)" — meaning that at Deep tier with ccc also present, ccc is supposed to be a transparent enhancement, not a tier driver. The step-01 logic produces the correct output for this case (Deep wins because it checks first), but the in-line comment on step-01 line 89 says "regardless of gh/qmd" — which is technically true but misleading. An agent reading that rule in isolation might incorrectly conclude Forge+ can override Deep. The `tier-rules.md` is explicit; step-01 is ambiguous.

**Risk:** An LLM executing step-01 sequentially could misread the evaluation order and assign Forge+ when the user actually qualifies for Deep (if all 4 tools are present but the agent evaluates the Forge+ rule before Deep for any reason — e.g., if it doesn't treat "first matching rule wins" as strict sequential evaluation).

**Fix:** Add a parenthetical guard to step-01 line 89: change `- {ast_grep} AND {ccc} both true (regardless of gh/qmd) → Forge+` to `- {ast_grep} AND {ccc} both true AND NOT ({gh_cli} AND {qmd}) → Forge+` — or add an explicit note: "(only reached if the Deep rule above did not match)."

---

### C-2: `shared/health-check.md` Path in step-04 Frontmatter Is Relative to the Wrong Anchor Point — Resolution Depends on Agent Behavior

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-04-report.md`, frontmatter line 3

```yaml
nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'
```

Step-04 lives at `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-04-report.md`. The reference `shared/health-check.md` has no `../` prefix, which makes it ambiguous: it could resolve to:
- `src/skf-setup/steps-c/shared/health-check.md` — does not exist
- `src/skf-setup/shared/health-check.md` — does not exist
- `src/shared/health-check.md` — exists and is the correct target

Every other workflow in the repo uses the same pattern (`shared/health-check.md`), and all their final-step files live in `steps-c/`. The actual file is at `src/shared/health-check.md`, two levels up from `steps-c/`. This means the reference only works if the agent resolves `shared/health-check.md` relative to the skill root (`src/skf-setup/`) rather than the file (`steps-c/`), or relative to the `src/` directory.

This is a cross-workflow consistency observation — the pattern is uniform across all 14 final-step files, so it is presumably intentional and tested to work. However, the correct relative path from `steps-c/` would be `../../shared/health-check.md`. The current pattern is either relying on a convention that paths are resolved from the skill root, or it is an unresolved ambiguity.

**Verdict:** Not blocking for v1.0.0 only if there is an established runtime convention that `shared/` paths are resolved from the module root rather than the calling file's directory. That convention is nowhere documented in skf-setup — it is implicit. If any agent resolves it relative to the file, it will silently fail to find health-check.md. Since this pattern affects every workflow equally, fixing it is a broader concern, but it should be documented somewhere explicit.

---

## HIGH Issues

### H-1: SKILL.md Invocation Contract Claims "Gates: none" But step-03 Has a Real User-Facing Gate

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/SKILL.md`, line 42; `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-03-auto-index.md`, section 4

The Invocation Contract table states `| **Gates** | (none) |`. But step-03 (QMD Collection Hygiene) presents a `[R]emove / [K]eep` prompt to the user when orphaned QMD collections are found. Step-03's own EXECUTION RULES section at the bottom correctly notes "This step has one optional user interaction (orphan removal prompt)."

This is a direct contradiction. The contract says no gates; the step has one. For headless mode users, the behavior is defined (SKILL.md line 25: "auto-proceed through confirmation gates with their default action"), but the contract misrepresents the workflow to users who read SKILL.md to understand what to expect. What is the default action for the orphan removal gate? It is not stated explicitly in step-03 or the SKILL.md contract.

**Fix:** Change the Gates row to `| **Gates** | One optional: orphaned QMD collection removal (step 3, Deep tier only) |` and add a default action annotation to step-03's orphan removal prompt (e.g., "Default: [K] Keep").

---

### H-2: step-03 Title and SKILL.md Stages Table Are Mismatched

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/SKILL.md` line 34; `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-03-auto-index.md` line 5

The Stages table in SKILL.md names step 3 "Auto Index". The actual step file's title (H1) is "Step 3: QMD Collection Hygiene". The step file is named `step-03-auto-index.md`, which compounds the confusion — the filename says "auto-index" but the step title and purpose is "QMD Collection Hygiene."

The step does no indexing at all. It cross-references the QMD collection registry and cleans stale entries. The name "Auto Index" appears to be a holdover from an earlier design. step-02's completion message ("Proceeding to QMD collection hygiene...") correctly uses the actual name, which creates an inconsistency with SKILL.md calling it "Auto Index."

**Fix:** Either rename the Stages table entry to "QMD Hygiene" (preferred) or rename the step file and update all references. The completion message in step-02 already uses the correct name and does not need changing.

---

### H-3: forger Template `forge-tier.yaml` Missing `file_count` and `exclude_patterns` Fields That the Setup Workflow Writes

**Location:** `src/forger/forge-tier.yaml` (lines 19–26); `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-02-write-config.md` (lines 48–51)

The canonical sidecar template at `src/forger/forge-tier.yaml` defines the `ccc_index` block as:

```yaml
ccc_index:
  indexed_path: ~
  last_indexed: ~
  status: "none"
  staleness_threshold_hours: 24
```

But step-02 writes two additional fields into this block:

```yaml
  file_count: {ccc_file_count from step-01b, or ~}
  exclude_patterns: {ccc_exclude_patterns from step-01b, or []}
```

Neither `file_count` nor `exclude_patterns` appears in the forger template. This means the template does not accurately represent the full schema of the written file. Any tool, consumer, or developer reading the template to understand the schema gets an incomplete picture. The `ccc-bridge.md` knowledge file does describe `ccc_index.exclude_patterns` and the staleness threshold, but the discrepancy with the canonical template is a documentation gap.

**Fix:** Add both fields to `src/forger/forge-tier.yaml` with `~` and `[]` defaults respectively.

---

### H-4: step-01b Section 3 Comment — "undefined (healthy daemon where no explicit state was recorded)" Is Internally Contradictory

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-01b-ccc-index.md`, section 3, line 89

The condition reads:

```
If {ccc_daemon} is "stopped" or undefined (healthy daemon where no explicit state was recorded)
```

`{ccc_daemon}` is set explicitly by step-01 with one of three values: `"healthy"`, `"stopped"`, or `"error"`. It is never undefined after step-01 runs. The parenthetical "(healthy daemon where no explicit state was recorded)" directly contradicts step-01's logic, which always records a state when `ccc` is true. If `ccc` is false, `ccc_daemon` is not set at all, but step-01b skips section 3 entirely in that case (the flow only reaches section 3 when `{needs_reindex}` is true, which only applies when `{ccc}` is true).

The comment appears to describe a legacy path or a defensive fallback. If the intent is to also proceed with indexing when the daemon state is `"healthy"` (the daemon is available, so indexing can start), then the condition should include `"healthy"` explicitly rather than the opaque "undefined" case.

**Fix:** Revise the condition to: `If {ccc_daemon} is "stopped", "healthy", or "error"` — or, since step-01b section 3 is only reached via the `{needs_reindex}` flag, simplify by removing the daemon condition header entirely and handling the `"error"` state with a single note: "Proceed in all daemon states; errors will be caught by the failure path below."

---

## MEDIUM Issues

### M-1: step-03 Forge+ Skip Comment References QMD Not Being Available, But the Check Is Actually Tier-Based

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-03-auto-index.md`, section 1, paragraph 2 of Rules

The Rules block states: "For Quick and Forge tiers (without ccc), skip silently and proceed. For Forge+ tier, skip QMD hygiene but the step routes correctly to the next step."

The parenthetical "(without ccc)" is incorrect. Quick and Forge tiers skip QMD hygiene because QMD is not available at those tiers — not because ccc is absent. The ccc availability is orthogonal to the QMD skip decision. Forge+ also lacks QMD (it requires only ast-grep + ccc), so the skip logic is tier-based, but the comment misleads by attributing the Quick/Forge skip to ccc absence.

Also, the Forge+ comment "skip QMD hygiene but the step routes correctly to the next step" is worded oddly — the step routes to section 5b (CCC Index Registry Hygiene), not directly to Auto-Proceed. This is a minor framing issue but could confuse a reader expecting a direct skip.

---

### M-2: step-04 `--update-spec` Implementation Is Underdefined for Production Use

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-04-report.md`, section 3 (lines 86–95)

The `--update-spec` section says "Use `gh` or `curl` to retrieve the spec" without specifying the URL to fetch from, the expected format, or what `agentskills-spec.json` should contain. The destination is `{forge_data_folder}/agentskills-spec.json` but the actual agentskills.io spec endpoint is not named. An LLM executing this step would have no verifiable target URL and would be forced to hallucinate or improvise the fetch location.

For a v1.0.0 release, if `--update-spec` is shipped as a documented feature, the URL must be explicit. If this is a placeholder for a future feature, the section should be marked `(reserved — not yet implemented)` to prevent agents from attempting a fabricated fetch.

---

### M-3: step-02 On Activation Resolves `project_name` but Module.yaml and skf-forger/SKILL.md Don't List It as a Module Variable

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/SKILL.md` line 49; `src/module.yaml`

`SKILL.md`'s On Activation block lists `project_name` as one of the variables to resolve from `config.yaml`. Looking at `module.yaml`, `project_name` is not defined as a module-level variable — the module defines `skills_output_folder`, `forge_data_folder`, `sidecar_path`, and `health_check_repo`, with a comment noting that `user_name`, `communication_language`, `document_output_language`, and `output_folder` come from Core Config. `project_name` is not listed in either place.

This is not necessarily a bug — `project_name` may come from the core BMAD config — but since it appears in On Activation's resolution list without any documentation of where it comes from, it is a potential source of confusion or a missing initialization path.

---

### M-4: step-01b Section 2b — CCC Exclusions Applied Before `ccc init` May Write a `settings.yml` That Gets Overwritten

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-01b-ccc-index.md`, sections 2b and 3

The flow in section 2b says: if `{settings_yml_existed}` is true, apply exclusions now. Then in section 3, it says: if `{settings_yml_existed}` is false (first-time setup), apply exclusions after `ccc init` creates the file.

The issue: section 2b checks for the file before `ccc init` runs. But if the file exists from a previous run and the user then runs `ccc init` again (in section 3), could `ccc init` overwrite `settings.yml` with defaults, discarding the exclusions just applied in section 2b? The step says "If init fails (project may already be initialized): continue — this is not an error," which implies a re-init scenario. If `ccc init` on a project that already has a CCC config does not overwrite `settings.yml`, this is fine. If it does, exclusions applied in section 2b are silently lost.

This requires verification against ccc's actual behavior. The step should add a note clarifying whether `ccc init` on an already-initialized project preserves `settings.yml`.

---

### M-5: SKILL.md Headless Resolution Order Differs Slightly from skf-forger/SKILL.md

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/SKILL.md` line 52; `src/skf-forger/SKILL.md` line 68

`skf-setup/SKILL.md` says headless is true if `--headless` or `-H` was passed, OR if `headless_mode: true` in preferences.yaml. But at the point SKILL.md runs On Activation step 2, preferences.yaml may not yet exist (first run). Step-02 creates preferences.yaml, which comes after step-01 which runs after On Activation. On a first run, checking `preferences.yaml` for `headless_mode` will return nothing — this is silently fine since the default is false, but the activation steps don't mention the "file may not exist" case explicitly.

`skf-forger/SKILL.md` always loads preferences.yaml as a Critical Action (before dispatching to skf-setup), so for normal usage through Ferris the file would already exist. But if skf-setup is invoked directly, the activation path could try to read a non-existent preferences.yaml. The steps handle this correctly in practice (step-01 explicitly handles "not found → set to null"), but SKILL.md's On Activation description implies preferences.yaml is always readable, which is only true on re-runs.

---

## LOW Issues

### L-1: step-02 Write Config Completion Note Says "execute the auto-index step" — Stale Label

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-02-write-config.md`, line 122

"ONLY WHEN forge-tier.yaml has been written successfully ... will you load and read fully `{nextStepFile}` to execute the auto-index step."

As noted in H-2, step-03 is QMD Collection Hygiene, not auto-indexing. This completion note preserves the old name. Low impact since it doesn't affect execution, but it will cause confusion for anyone reading the completion notes as documentation.

### L-2: step-04 Report Format Template — CCC Index Display Block Uses `{if tools.ccc is true}` But the Variable Spelling Varies

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-04-report.md`, line 59; `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-03-auto-index.md`, line 106

Step-04 report template uses `{if tools.ccc is true}` (without underscore in `tools.ccc`). Step-03 section 5b uses `IF tools.ccc is true in forge-tier.yaml`. These match. But step-02's YAML template writes the key as `ccc:` under `tools:`, and the forge-tier.yaml template uses `ccc:` as well. The context variable across step instructions uses `{ccc}` (from step-01) but the YAML key is `tools.ccc`. This is consistent within the overall schema but the context variable vs YAML key naming is easy to confuse: `{ccc}` (boolean context variable from step-01) versus `tools.ccc` (YAML path in forge-tier.yaml) are two different things used in different scopes.

This is documentation clarity, not a bug. Adding a short note in step-02 that "`{ccc}` from step-01 context maps to `tools.ccc` in forge-tier.yaml" would reduce confusion.

---

### L-3: `templates/` and `assets/` Directories Do Not Exist — SKILL.md Description References Neither

**Location:** Glob result; `src/skf-setup/`

The skf-setup skill has no `templates/` or `assets/` subdirectory — only `steps-c/` and `references/`. This is not a problem since SKILL.md does not reference either. Noting it only to confirm the review scope is complete: there are no missing assets referenced from SKILL.md.

---

### L-4: step-04 — "agentskills.io spec" Terminology Is Slightly Ambiguous

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-04-report.md`, section 3

The stored file is named `agentskills-spec.json`, but the `src/knowledge/agentskills-spec.md` knowledge fragment defines the spec as a YAML frontmatter schema, not a JSON object. The `--update-spec` feature stores to `.json` but the spec documented in the knowledge base is `yaml`-adjacent. This is not a blocking issue but the format choice should be deliberate and documented.

## CRITICAL Issues

### C-1: Tier Logic Conflict Between step-01 and tier-rules.md — Forge+ Can Incorrectly Win Over Deep

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-01-detect-and-tier.md`, section 8 (lines 87–91)

**The problem:** Step-01's tier evaluation list reads:

```
- {ast_grep} AND {gh_cli} AND {qmd} all true → Deep
- {ast_grep} AND {ccc} both true (regardless of gh/qmd) → Forge+
- {ast_grep} true → Forge
- Otherwise → Quick
```

Because the rule says "first matching rule wins," a user with all four tools installed (ast-grep + gh + qmd + ccc) correctly hits Deep first. So far so good. But if a user has ast-grep + ccc + gh (no qmd), the Forge+ rule correctly fires next. That path is also fine.

The conflict is semantic, not computational: the comment in `tier-rules.md` line 33 states "ast-grep + gh + qmd + ccc → Deep (Deep subsumes Forge+; ccc enhances transparently)" — meaning that at Deep tier with ccc also present, ccc is supposed to be a transparent enhancement, not a tier driver. The step-01 logic produces the correct output for this case (Deep wins because it checks first), but the in-line comment on step-01 line 89 says "regardless of gh/qmd" — which is technically true but misleading. An agent reading that rule in isolation might incorrectly conclude Forge+ can override Deep. The `tier-rules.md` is explicit; step-01 is ambiguous.

**Risk:** An LLM executing step-01 sequentially could misread the evaluation order and assign Forge+ when the user actually qualifies for Deep (if all 4 tools are present but the agent evaluates the Forge+ rule before Deep for any reason — e.g., if it doesn't treat "first matching rule wins" as strict sequential evaluation).

**Fix:** Add a parenthetical guard to step-01 line 89: change `- {ast_grep} AND {ccc} both true (regardless of gh/qmd) → Forge+` to `- {ast_grep} AND {ccc} both true AND NOT ({gh_cli} AND {qmd}) → Forge+` — or add an explicit note: "(only reached if the Deep rule above did not match)."

---

### C-2: `shared/health-check.md` Path in step-04 Frontmatter Is Relative to the Wrong Anchor Point — Resolution Depends on Agent Behavior

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-04-report.md`, frontmatter line 3

```yaml
nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'
```

Step-04 lives at `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-04-report.md`. The reference `shared/health-check.md` has no `../` prefix, which makes it ambiguous: it could resolve to:
- `src/skf-setup/steps-c/shared/health-check.md` — does not exist
- `src/skf-setup/shared/health-check.md` — does not exist
- `src/shared/health-check.md` — exists and is the correct target

Every other workflow in the repo uses the same pattern (`shared/health-check.md`), and all their final-step files live in `steps-c/`. The actual file is at `src/shared/health-check.md`, two levels up from `steps-c/`. This means the reference only works if the agent resolves `shared/health-check.md` relative to the skill root (`src/skf-setup/`) rather than the file (`steps-c/`), or relative to the `src/` directory.

This is a cross-workflow consistency observation — the pattern is uniform across all 14 final-step files, so it is presumably intentional and tested to work. However, the correct relative path from `steps-c/` would be `../../shared/health-check.md`. The current pattern is either relying on a convention that paths are resolved from the skill root, or it is an unresolved ambiguity.

**Verdict:** Not blocking for v1.0.0 only if there is an established runtime convention that `shared/` paths are resolved from the module root rather than the calling file's directory. That convention is nowhere documented in skf-setup — it is implicit. If any agent resolves it relative to the file, it will silently fail to find health-check.md. Since this pattern affects every workflow equally, fixing it is a broader concern, but it should be documented somewhere explicit.

---

## HIGH Issues

**Location:** `src/skf-setup/SKILL.md`, line 42; `src/skf-setup/steps-c/step-03-auto-index.md`, section 4

This is a direct contradiction. The contract says no gates; the step has one. For headless mode users, the behavior is defined (SKILL.md line 25: "auto-proceed through confirmation gates with their default action"), but the contract misrepresents the workflow to users who read SKILL.md to understand what to expect. What is the default action for the orphan removal gate? It is not stated explicitly in step-03 or the SKILL.md contract.

**Fix:** Change the Gates row to `| **Gates** | One optional: orphaned QMD collection removal (step 3, Deep tier only) |` and add a default action annotation to step-03's orphan removal prompt (e.g., "Default: [K] Keep").

The step does no indexing at all. It cross-references the QMD collection registry and cleans stale entries.
